items.title: Brown-Séquard: spinal hemisection
items.contentHtml:
Brown-Séquard syndrome

  • Physiopathology: unilateral spinal hemisection that interrupts the lateral corticospinal tract, lateral spinothalamic tract and ipsilateral posterior cord.
  • Clinic: ipsilateral loss of motility (spastic paralysis) and proprioception/vibration below the lesion; contralateral loss of thermoalgesic sensitivity (pain and temperature) 1-2 segments below.
  • Classic cause: penetrating trauma (stab or projectile wound), extramedullary tumors or multiple sclerosis.
  • Askable fact: the sensory level is usually asymmetric; The plantar cutaneous reflex is ipsilateral extensor.

items.title: Anterior spinal cord syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Anterior spinal cord syndrome

  • Pathophysiology: injury to the anterior 2/3 of the spinal cord (anterior spinal artery) that affects the corticospinal and spinothalamic tracts, sparing the posterior cords.
  • Clinic: bilateral motor paralysis and loss of thermoalgesic sensitivity below the injury; fine touch, vibration and proprioception are preserved (posterior cords intact).
  • Most common cause: ischemia due to hypoperfusion (e.g. cardiac arrest, ruptured aortic aneurysm) or trauma due to cervical hyperextension.
  • Prognosis: the worst of the incomplete spinal cord syndromes; motor recovery is poor.

items.title: Central spinal cord syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Central spinal cord syndrome

  • Pathophysiology: injury to the center of the spinal cord (gray matter and crossing spinothalamic fibers), typically due to cervical hyperextension in patients with preexisting cervical stenosis.
  • Clinical: greater motor weakness in the upper limbs than in the lower limbs (distal > proximal); There may be thermoalgesic loss in a “hood” pattern (suspended band).
  • Key fact: It is the most common incomplete syndrome in cervical trauma; sacral sensitivity is usually preserved.
  • Prognosis: recovery begins with lower extremities and bladder function; better prognosis than the previous spinal cord syndrome.

items.title: Posterior cord syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Posterior cordonal syndrome

  • Physiopathology: isolated lesion of the posterior cords (gracile and cuneiform fascicles) that transmit conscious proprioception, vibration and epicritic touch.
  • Clinical: sensory ataxia (positive Romberg sign), loss of vibratory and proprioceptive sensitivity below the lesion; motor strength and thermoalgesic sensitivity are preserved.
  • Classic causes: tabes dorsalis (neurosyphilis), vitamin B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration), multiple sclerosis or posterior extrinsic compression.
  • Examination finding: the gait is heeling (steppage is due to peripheral neuropathy, do not confuse).

items.title: Conus medullaris syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Conus medullaris syndrome

  • Anatomy: the conus medullaris is located in L1-L2; Its lesion affects sacral centers (S2-S4) of bladder, intestinal and sexual control.
  • Clinic: early sphincter dysfunction (urinary retention and fecal incontinence), saddle anesthesia (perineal and sacral region) and loss of bulbocavernosus and anal reflexes; the motor function of the lower extremities is usually preserved at the beginning.
  • Differential with cauda equina: the onset is sudden, symmetrical and with absent reflexes; there is no intense radicular pain.
  • Etiologies: central disc herniation, fracture-dislocation of L1, tumor (ependymoma) or myelitis.

items.title: Cauda equina syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Caquina equina syndrome

  • Anatomy: injury to the lumbosacral nerve roots below L1 (cauda equina); It is a lower motor neuron lesion.
  • Clinic: Intense, asymmetrical lumbar and radicular pain, with flaccid weakness of the lower extremities, Achilles and patellar areflexia, saddle anesthesia and late bladder dysfunction.
  • Key fact: The onset is gradual and progressive; bulbocavernosus and anal reflexes may be decreased or absent.
  • Surgical urgency: requires decompression <24-48 hours to avoid permanent deficit; It is considered a neurosurgical emergency.

items.foco: ASIA scale for spinal cord injury
items.title: ASIA Scale (A-E)
items.contentHtml:
ASIA Scale for spinal cord injury

  • Definition: American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) Impairment Scale classifies the degree of motor and sensory deficit in spinal cord injuries.
  • Grades: A = complete (without motor or sensory function in sacral segments S4-S5); B = incomplete sensory (preserves sacral sensitivity but without motor function below the level); C = motor incomplete with muscle strength <3/5 in more than half of key muscles below level; D = incomplete motor with strength >=3/5 in at least half; E = normal.
  • Questionable fact: the presence of deep anal sensation or voluntary contraction of the anal sphincter classifies as incomplete (B or higher).

items.title: Spinal cord vs neurogenic shock
items.contentHtml:
Spinal cord shock vs neurogenic

  • Spinal cord shock: transient loss of all reflexes below the injury (areflexia, flaccidity, absence of bulbocavernosus reflex) that lasts days to weeks; it is not a hemodynamic shock.
  • Neurogenic shock: hypotension due to loss of sympathetic tone (vasodilation) plus relative bradycardia; It occurs in high spinal cord injuries (above T6).
  • Key differential: in spinal shock there is areflexia and flaccidity; in neurogenic shock there is hypotension with bradycardia and hot/dry skin.
  • Management of neurogenic shock: intravenous fluids with caution (risk of pulmonary edema), first-line vasopressors: norepinephrine or phenylephrine.

items.title: Key sensory and motor level
items.contentHtml:
Key sensory and motor level

  • Sensitive level: the most caudal dermatome with normal bilateral sensitivity (prick and light touch). Key points include: C2 (occipital protuberance), T4 (nipple), T10 (navel), L1 (groin), S4-S5 (perianal region).
  • Motor level: the most caudal myotome with strength >=3/5 in the key muscle. Key muscles: C5 (elbow flexors), C6 (wrist extensors), C7 (elbow extensors), L2 (hip flexors), L3 (knee extensors), S1 (ankle plantar flexors).
  • Rule: in complete injury, the motor and sensory level are the same or differ by 1 segment.

items.title: Bulbocavernosus reflex prognosis
items.contentHtml:
Bulbocavernosus reflex prognosis

  • Definition: contraction of the anal sphincter when stimulating the glans (or clitoris) or when pulling on the bladder catheter; reflex arc S2-S4.
  • Prognostic importance: its reappearance marks the end of spinal cord shock (areflexia phase). Once it reappears, the actual neurological deficit can be evaluated.
  • Key fact: if the bulbocavernosus reflex is present from the beginning, the injury is complete (ASIA A) and the prognosis for motor recovery is poor.
  • In incomplete injury: the early presence of the reflex does not contraindicate recovery; persistent absence >24 h suggests serious injury.

items.title: Cervical Horner syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Horner syndrome in cervical injury

  • Pathophysiology: interruption of the cervical sympathetic pathway (ciliospinal center at C8-T1, cervical sympathetic chain) that produces ptosis, miosis (pupillary constriction), ipsilateral facial anhidrosis and enophthalmos.
  • Location: lower cervical or upper thoracic spinal cord injury (C8-T1); it is a sign of complete or severe injury at that level.
  • Questionable fact: in cervical trauma, ipsilateral Horner's triad + contralateral motor deficit suggests high Brown-Séquard syndrome.
  • Pharmacological test: drops of apraclonidine or hydroxyamphetamine confirm preganglionic denervation (pupillary dilation <0.5 mm).

items.foco: Corticosteroids in acute spinal cord trauma
items.title: Corticosteroids in spinal cord trauma
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids in acute spinal cord trauma

  • Controversial indication: based on the NASCIS II study (National Acute Spinal Cord Injury Study), which showed benefit if methylprednisolone was administered as a bolus of 30 mg/kg intravenously in 15 minutes, followed by an infusion of 5.4 mg/kg/hour for 23 hours.
  • Conditions: It must be started within the first 8 hours post-injury; if started between 3-8 hours, the infusion is extended to 48 hours.
  • Current controversy: not standard of care; It is associated with a higher risk of infections and complications; Current guidelines (Congress of Neurological Surgeons) do not recommend its routine use.
  • Exam data: In practice, it is considered optional and only in selected patients without contraindications.
